name: Dual-Process Algebraic Drive Model
description: Algebraic computation of a composite intake drive from separate homeostatic and hedonic signals with a multiplicative interaction term, used directly to threshold action selection. The total intake drive I(t) integrates an independent homeostatic hunger signal H(t), a hedonic liking signal L(t) based on food palatability, and a wanting signal W(t) that multiplicatively combines reward value with motivational state. This follows the dual-process architecture from Lutter & Nestler (2009) and AJPH (2014), with the incentive-salience formulation from Toates (1986) and Berridge (2004). The agent eats when drive exceeds a threshold, approaches food when drive is moderate, and stays/wanders when drive is low.

variables.name: homeostatic_hunger
variables.description: Energy deficit proxy; increases each timestep without eating, resets to a low residual value upon eating

variables.name: hedonic_reward_signal
variables.description: Palatability-driven hedonic value of nearest visible food, decaying with distance (sensory cue salience)

variables.name: wanting_incentive_salience
variables.description: Motivational drive toward food; product of reward value and motivational state (Toates/Berridge incentive salience)

variables.name: liking_hedonic_impact
variables.description: Anticipated pleasure of consumption; primarily driven by palatability but modulated upward by hunger

variables.name: total_intake_drive
variables.description: Composite signal from homeostatic, hedonic liking, and wanting components that determines whether the agent seeks or consumes food

variables.name: distance_to_nearest_food
variables.description: Manhattan distance on the grid to the nearest visible food resource

variables.name: palatability_of_nearest_food
variables.description: Intrinsic hedonic value of the nearest food item from env_spec palatability property

parameters.name: homeostatic_weight
parameters.source: Derived from dual-process balance in Lutter & Nestler (2009): hedonic can override homeostatic, so beta > alpha

parameters.name: hedonic_weight
parameters.source: Reflects hedonic dominance in palatable-food environments (Lutter & Nestler, 2009)

parameters.name: hunger_growth_rate
parameters.source: Calibrated to ~20 steps to reach strong hunger; biological hunger accumulation rate

parameters.name: eat_threshold
parameters.source: Tuning parameter: agent eats when total intake drive exceeds this

parameters.name: seek_threshold
parameters.source: Below eat threshold but above this: agent moves toward food

parameters.name: post_meal_hunger_reset
parameters.source: Small residual hunger after eating, reflecting incomplete satiation

parameters.name: sensory_cue_distance_decay
parameters.source: Sensory cue salience decays with distance; food cues stronger when proximal (Kelley & Berridge, 2002)

rules.description: Hunger accumulation: hunger increases linearly each step without eating, capped at 1.0
rules.pseudocode: if not ate_last_step: H = min(1.0, H + lambda_H)

rules.description: Hunger reset upon eating: hunger drops to a small residual after successful consumption
Hedonic reward signal: palatability of nearest food decayed by Manhattan distance, modeling sensory cue salience
rules.source_postulate: P1, P6 (sensory inputs trigger hedonic circuits independently of metabolic state; Kelley & Berridge, 2002)

rules.description: Wanting (incentive salience): multiplicative interaction of reward value and hunger state (Toates/Berridge formulation)
rules.source_postulate: P2 (wanting is dissociable component; Berridge, 2004; Toates, 1986)

rules.description: Liking (hedonic impact): primarily palatability-driven with hunger modulation and a floor ensuring palatable food is liked even when sated
rules.source_postulate: P1, P2 (liking is dissociable; palatability alone is sufficient to drive some consumption)

rules.description: Total intake drive: dual-process composite of homeostatic, hedonic liking, and wanting terms (AJPH, 2014 model)
rules.pseudocode: I = alpha * H + beta * L + gamma * W
rules.source_postulate: P3 (hedonic pathway can override homeostatic satiety); P4 (hunger amplifies reward)

decision_logic.description: Threshold-based action selection using total intake drive I(t). Agent eats if on food and drive is high, approaches food if drive is moderate, or wanders/stays when drive is low. When no food is visible, the agent performs a random walk.
pseudocode: # 1. Update hunger H based on whether agent ate last step
if last_action_result indicates successful eat: H = H_reset
if len(foods) == 0: nearest = None; d_food = 999; p_food = 0.0
for each f in foods: dist = abs(f.x - perception.x) + abs(f.y - perception.y)
I = alpha * H + beta * L + gamma * W
elif I >= theta_seek and nearest is not None:
# Move toward nearest food (greedy Manhattan-distance reduction)
# Low drive: random walk or stay

perception_to_variables.food_sources: perception.resources.food  # list of {x, y, type, palatability}
perception_to_variables.ate_last_step: last_action was 'eat' and reward > 0 (from update method's reward parameter)

env_mapping.reward_source: eat action → ConsumeEffect reward of 1.0 when food is at agent position

expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: run 20 steps with no food on grid → assert H increases by ~lambda_H per step toward 1.0

expected_behaviors.description: Hedonic overeating: agent eats highly palatable food even at low hunger (P1, P3)
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set H = 0.1, place food with palatability=1.0 at agent position → L = 1.0*(0.6+0.04)=0.64, I = 0.04+0.32+... > theta_eat → agent eats

expected_behaviors.description: Low-palatability food ignored when sated: agent does not eat bland food unless hungry
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set H = 0.1, place food with palatability=0.1 at agent → I = alpha*0.1 + beta*0.1*0.64 + gamma*small ≈ 0.07 < theta_eat → agent does NOT eat

expected_behaviors.description: Hunger amplifies food seeking: hungry agent approaches food from greater distance
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set H = 0.9, food at distance 5 with palatability=0.5 → I is larger than when H=0.1 → agent seeks food

expected_behaviors.description: Sensory cue decay: distant food produces weaker hedonic signal than proximal food
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: compare R for d_food=1 vs d_food=8 with same palatability → R(d=1) >> R(d=8)

references: Lutter & Nestler (2009) — Homeostatic and Hedonic Signals Interact in the Regulation of Food Intake — Journal of Nutrition (PMC2714382)
AJPH (2014) — Dynamic Interplay Among Homeostatic, Hedonic, and Cognitive Feedback Circuits Regulating Body Weight — doi:10.2105/AJPH.2014.301931
Toates (1986) — Incentive theory of motivation — as cited in ScienceDirect S0195666322002379
Berridge (2004) — Incentive theory of motivation applied to ingestive behavior — as cited in ScienceDirect S0195666322002379
Berridge & Robinson (1998) — What is the role of dopamine in reward: hedonic impact, reward learning, and incentive salience? — Brain Research Reviews (PubMed 9858756)
Kelley & Berridge (2002) — The Need to Feed: Homeostatic and Hedonic Control of Eating — Neuron (ScienceDirect S0896627302009698)
